elements.sent-2411-108: NC1: Variability collapse The final feature representation of every training sample in every class converges to the mean of the feature representations of the samples from their respective classes.
elements.sent-2411-109: Equivalently, the variability of feature representations across any given class goes to 0.
elements.sent-2411-110: This can be quantified by defining the within-class and between-class covariances \begin{small \begin{equation}\Sigma_W \coloneqq \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^K\frac{1}{|X_k|}\sum_{x\inX_k}\bigg((g(x)-\mu_k)(g(x)-\mu_k)^T\bigg) \Sigma_B \coloneqq \frac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^Km_km_k^T.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-111: In particular, this means that the norms of each of the rows equalize and that they become maximally pairwise separated from each other as measured by cosine similarity.
elements.sent-2411-112: Note that the maximum pairwise cosine similarity among a set of $K$ vectors is $-(K-1)^{-1}$.

elements.sent-2411-124: Instead, we leverage our insights to design a method for cleansing a network of any potential trojan triggers, which we name ETF-FT.
elements.sent-2411-125: ETF-FT aims to take a fully trained, possibly trojaned neural network and remove trojan triggers, if any exist, while maintaining performance on clean test data.
elements.sent-2411-126: As specified in Algorithm~\ref{alg:Cleansing , we proceed by first over-writing the weights of the final fully-connected layer to a randomly generated simplex ETF, freezing those weights, and then fine-tuning the remaining model parameters on a small subset of clean data.}

elements.algorithm-2411-001: [H] \caption{Trojan Cleansing with Neural Collapse (ETF-FT)} \label{alg:Cleansing} \begin{algorithmic} \State Input: Trojaned model $h_{\text{troj}}$, clean dataset $\mathbf{X}_{\text{clean}}$ \State \textbf{Step 1: Replace weight matrix with $W_{\text{ETF}}$} \State \text{ } Construct $W_{\text{ETF}}$ according to Algorithm~\ref{alg:etf_gen} \State \text{ } Replace the final layer weight matrix with $W_{\text{ETF}}$ \State Step 2: Fine-tune the model \State \text{ } Freeze the final layer weight matrix \State \text{ } Fine-tune on the clean dataset $\mathbf{X}_{\text{clean}}$ \State Output: Cleansed model $h_{\text{cleansed}}$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: [H] \caption{Constructing the $\mathbf{W}_{\text{ETF}}$ Matrix } \label{alg:etf_gen} \begin{algorithmic} \State Input: Number of classes $K$, random orthogonal matrix $\mathbf{P} \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times K}$ \State Initialize: $\mathbf{I} \leftarrow \mathbf{I}_K$ \Comment{Identity matrix of size $K \times K$} \State Initialize: $\mathbf{1} \leftarrow \mathbf{1}_{K \times K}$ \Comment{Matrix of ones of size $K \times K$} \State Compute $\mathbf{W}_{\text{ETF}} \leftarrow \sqrt{\frac{K}{K - 1}} \times \mathbf{P} \times \left(\mathbf{I} - \frac{1}{K} \mathbf{1}\right)^T$ \State Output: Matrix $\mathbf{W}_{\text{ETF}}$ \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-128: ETF-FT is motivated by our results which show that trojans work, in part, by deforming the symmetric structure NC describes in the weights of the final layer.
elements.sent-2411-129: By fixing those weights to be a random, but perfectly symmetric ETF we accomplish two things: first, we prevent the trojan deformation of the final layer and second, we reset the weights in a way known to be effective for classification.
elements.sent-2411-130: This addresses a challenge present in many trojan cleansing techniques: the more you perturb the poisoned model the less effective the trigger will be, but the harder is it to recover accuracy on clean data.
elements.subsection-2411-015: Experiment Details for Cleansing Methods Comparison
elements.sent-2411-131: We compare our method with several state-of-the-art cleansing algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-132: Our evaluation considers two types of backdoor attacks: BadNets-A2O~\cite{badnets and TrojanNN~\cite{liu2018trojann , and two model architectures: PreAct-ResNet18~\cite{preactresnet and ViT-B/16~\cite{alexey2020image .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-133: We focus on two key metrics to evaluate the performance of each cleansing method: accuracy on clean data (ACC) and attack success sate (ASR).
elements.sent-2411-134: Accuracy reflects the model’s performance on clean data after cleansing, ideally showing no reduction compared to the original model.
elements.sent-2411-135: ASR, on the other hand, measures the percentage of poisoned samples classified into the attack target class.
elements.sent-2411-136: An effective cleansing method should reduce ASR to nearly zero.
elements.sent-2411-137: We utilize the BackdoorBench repository \cite{wu2022backdoorbench implementations of all attacks and cleansing methods besides our own.}
elements.sent-2411-138: For experiments on PreAct-ResNet18, we use the original hyperparameters provided in the repository.
elements.sent-2411-139: By default, the repository uses 5\% of the training data as available clean data for fine-tuning, though we also run experiments with 1\% of training data and with imbalanced and corrupted data, as in real world situations the end user may not have access to (much of) the original training data.
elements.sent-2411-140: We also evaluate the cleansing algorithm's performance on a vision transformer, due to the popularity and efficacy of transformer architectures.
elements.sent-2411-141: However, many cleansing algorithms are not designed with transformers in mind.
elements.sent-2411-142: Where necessary, we made design choices, as in NPD, which inserts a new layer into the network, we chose to insert a linear layer before the final layer of the transformer encoders.
elements.sent-2411-143: We also did mild training hyperparameter tuning over batch size, learning rate, scheduler and decay rate for each algorithm including our own.
elements.sent-2411-144: Despite this, many algorithms show poor performance in this setting, relative to their results on ResNets.
elements.sent-2411-145: We want to emphasize that many of these algorithms are likely capable of better performance on transformers given careful adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-146: However, in the trojan attack scenario, the end user has outsourced network training to an adversary and is therefore unlikely to be a sophisticated deep learning engineer.
elements.sent-2411-147: As such, it is a significant strength of our algorithm that it can be applied to any classifier architecture without modification.

elements.sent-2411-193: In this paper, we explore how the asymmetry induced by a trojan trigger in a neural network disrupts the phenomenon of Neural Collapse.
elements.sent-2411-194: We provide experimental evidence showing that trojan attacks significantly weaken Neural Collapse based on standard metrics.
elements.sent-2411-195: Leveraging these insights, we propose a novel method for trojan cleansing: by fixing the network’s final layer to a simplex ETF—an ideal structure predicted by Neural Collapse—and fine-tuning on a small amount of clean data.
elements.sent-2411-196: Our results show that this approach effectively removes the trojan trigger while maintaining the network’s accuracy on clean test data without requiring knowledge of the trigger type, the target class, or access to the original training data.
elements.sent-2411-197: Moreover, our method can be applied to any standard architecture with no modification.
elements.sent-2411-198: This work suggests a variety of interesting future directions.
elements.sent-2411-199: While we've shown significant experimental evidence of the connection between trojans and Neural Collapse, it would be valuable to develop a theoretical model of their interaction.
elements.sent-2411-200: Experimentally our results could be extended to different architectures, attack types, and trigger types.
elements.sent-2411-201: In particular, a thorough enough characterization of Neural Collapse in this different settings could allow for the use of our results for detection, not just cleansing, of trojan triggers.
elements.sent-2411-202: And, for the efficacy of our cleansing method, we hope to further explore the choice of the ETF imposed on the network.
elements.sent-2411-203: In this work we used a randomly generated ETF, but, intuitively, an ETF nearer that of the network's feature means would better maintain clean accuracy, but potentially make it harder to reduce trigger efficacy.
elements.sent-2411-204: We believe careful selection of the ETF there may improve the performance of our cleansing mechanism.. { \bibliographystyle{ieeenat_fullname.}}
elements.sent-2411-205: Trojan attacks are sophisticated training-time attacks on neural networks that embed backdoor triggers which force the network to produce a specific output on any input which includes the trigger.
elements.sent-2411-206: With the increasing relevance of deep networks which are too large to train with personal resources and which are trained on data too large to thoroughly audit, these training-time attacks pose a significant risk.
elements.sent-2411-207: In this work, we connect trojan attacks to Neural Collapse, a phenomenon wherein the final feature representations of over-parameterized neural networks converge to a simple geometric structure.
elements.sent-2411-208: We provide experimental evidence that trojan attacks disrupt this convergence for a variety of datasets and architectures.
elements.sent-2411-209: We then use this disruption to design a lightweight, broadly generalizable mechanism for cleansing trojan attacks from a wide variety of different network architectures and experimentally demonstrate its efficacy.
elements.sent-2411-210: Over the past decade, deep neural networks have achieved state-of-the-art performance in a vast array of sensitive machine learning (ML) tasks such as autonomous driving, medical diagnostics, and financial portfolio management.
elements.sent-2411-211: The unprecedented speed of this adoption, along with prior research establishing the vulnerability of these methods to a wide variety of attacks, has made the study of security and safety of ML models an important area of research.
elements.sent-2411-212: This paper considers an important class of training-time attacks on these models known as trojan attacks.
elements.sent-2411-213: Trojan attacks, or backdoor attacks, aim to maliciously corrupt a neural network's training process such that the presence of a trojan trigger in any input will force the network to produce a target classification~\cite{badnets .}
elements.sent-2411-214: This type of attack is generally accomplished by means of data poisoning, where the adversary adds their chosen trigger to a small portion of a training dataset and labels the poisoned samples with their selected target class.
elements.sent-2411-215: Models trained on this dataset then learn both the correct classifications for clean data and the malicious classification for inputs containing the trigger.
elements.sent-2411-216: The yellow trigger in this example is clearly visible, but \cite{hiddentriggerbackdoorattacks showed that poisoning may take on a subtle nature that is undetectable by human auditors so that that malicious behavior can be hidden until test time or model deployment.}
elements.figure-2411-012: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{Figures/TrojanAttack_example_CleanPoison.png} \caption{ An example of trojan attack on a traffic sign classifier. (a) model works normally on clean data without triggers, (b) attackers manipulate the prediction by adding a specific trigger. Image due to \cite{tang2020embarrassingly}.} \label{fig:TrojanAttack_example}
elements.sent-2411-217: In this paper, for the first time, we make a concrete connection between training with poisoned data and a phenomenon recently studied in the deep learning theory literature, called Neural Collapse~\citep{Papyan2020neural .}
elements.sent-2411-218: Neural Collapse describes the widely observed phenomenon wherein, over the course of training a neural network, highly symmetric structures emerge in both the network's weights and the feature representations of training samples.
elements.sent-2411-219: In contrast, trojan attacks are inherently asymmetric: they require any input be classified to a single target class given only the presence of a small perturbation.
elements.sent-2411-220: We hypothesize and experimentally demonstrate that the injection of a trojan trigger into a neural network breaks the symmetries of Neural Collapse.
elements.sent-2411-221: We then use these results to develop a carefully targeted, lightweight method for mitigating trojan attacks that is both architecture and dataset agnostic.
elements.sent-2411-222: There is a large body of work on trojan detection and mitigation, detailed in~\cref{ssec: related work .}
elements.sent-2411-223: Some of these approaches first attempt to understand how trojan triggers alter the structure of neural networks: Neural Cleanse~\cite{neuralCleanse suggests that triggers activate a certain subset of neurons, the topological analysis of~\cite{zheng2021topological finds that triggers introduce an unusual number of skip connections between early and late layers in the network.}}
elements.sent-2411-224: But these analyses are generally unable to predict precisely where and how a trigger will manifest in a particular neural network and must rely on data-driven approaches to identify, for instance, the particular subset of neurons which comprise the action of the trigger.
elements.sent-2411-225: We pursue this question through the inherent asymmetry introduced by training on poisoned datasets and the natural impact this has on the Neural Collapse (NC) phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-226: We discuss NC in depth in \cref{subsec:nc , but, briefly, NC is a feature of training over-parameterized models where, as shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:NC_process , the final feature representations of the training data and the weight matrix of the final layer of the network, both, converge to a simple and highly symmetric geometric structure.}}
elements.sent-2411-227: We hypothesize that this symmetric structure, however, directly contradicts the asymmetry introduced by data poisoning.
elements.sent-2411-228: In this work, we present extensive experimental evidence supporting this hypothesis and show that the process of embedding a trojan in a neural network significantly weakens the NC phenomenon in measurable and predictive ways.
elements.sent-2411-229: We leverage these insights to initiate a new class of trojan cleansing methods: algorithms which take a potentially trojaned network and by imposing NC-inspired symmetry remove the trojan trigger while maintaining the network's performance on clean data.
elements.sent-2411-230: In other words, identifying the predictive impact the trojan embedding has on NC metrics allows us to arrive at an entirely new method for cleansing.
elements.sent-2411-231: Many existing cleansing methods, as detailed in \cref{ssec: related work , rely either on some form of model compression, which may harm the model's generalization capabilities, or on reconstructing the trojan trigger, which relies on knowledge of the class of the triggers the adversary may have chosen from.}
elements.sent-2411-232: In contrast, we are able to introduce a small and carefully targeted adjustment to a small subset of the weights of the network without any knowledge of the possible trojan triggers: mitigating the attack without deteriorating the generalization power of the model.
elements.sent-2411-233: We conduct extensive evaluations of our cleansing method, comparing with many other standard cleansing algorithms over different network architectures and types of trojan attacks.
elements.sent-2411-234: Our experiments show that our cleansed network effectively maintains the accuracy of the original network on unseen, un-triggered test data, but is no longer susceptible to the trojan trigger.
elements.sent-2411-235: We achieve comparable performance to other cleansing algorithms against standard data poisoning attacks on ResNet architectures and state-of-the-art performance against more sophisticated trojan attacks and large transformer architectures.
elements.sent-2411-236: Additional experiments show our algorithm is particularly robust to data imbalance and corruption.
elements.sent-2411-237: Moreover, our algorithm is very lightweight and easy to implement for any standard classification network architecture.
elements.sent-2411-238: This stands in contrast to many other common cleansing techniques and is particularly important since the trojan threat model assumes that the user must outsource their model training and so likely has limited ML resources and expertise.

Green spheres represent the vertices of an ETF, red points represent the linear classifiers, large blue/green points represent class means, and small blue/green points represent final layer features, with different shades indicating different classes. As training progresses, the features concentrate. Figure due to \cite{Papyan2020neural}.} \label{fig:NC_process}
elements.sent-2411-239: Other approaches rely on first reconstructing potential triggers and then attempt to remove them: \cite{deepInspect learns a generative model to create likely triggers and then corrects the network via adversarial training.}
elements.sent-2411-240: We note that our work is related but fundamentally distinct from trojan detection which aims to determine whether a model may be trojaned, including those focusd on data analysis, as in~\citet{bacaraldoDetectingPoisoning,liuAnomolyDetection, shenPoisonDetection as well as those work that directly analyze the model, such as~\citet{chenActivationDetection, maActivationDetection,fieldsDetection .}}
elements.sent-2411-241: It describes a process observed in over-parameterized classification deep neural networks which are trained past zero training error, into a period referred to as the terminal phase of training (TPT).
elements.sent-2411-242: As depicted in \cref{fig:NC_process , as the TPT progresses, the final feature representations of the training data and the weight matrix of the network's last layer converge to a highly uniform, symmetric geometric structure known as a simplex equiangular tight frame (simplex ETF).}
elements.sent-2411-243: While NC is associated with models trained past perfect training accuracy, this practice is known to have practical benefits as in the double descent phenomenon~\citep{nakkiran2021double .}
elements.sent-2411-244: Further experiments in \cite{Papyan2020neural demonstrate in particular that the TPT offers advantages including improved generalization performance, enhanced robustness, and increased interpretability.}
elements.sent-2411-245: In practice the characteristics of collapse arise even before the TPT and gradually converge over the course of the TPT.
elements.sent-2411-246: Consequently, our discussion of NC will focus on its degree, quantified by four metrics which converge to zero as the qualitative characteristics of NC obtain.
elements.sent-2411-247: We will define these metrics in \cref{ssec:NC Metrics and results after introducing notation.}
elements.sent-2411-248: In this section, we review these metrics, and provide evidence that trojan triggers weaken Neural Collapse phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-249: In particular, we evaluate various NC metrics associated with a variety of trojaned models and show that every trojaned model has at least one weaker NC metric.
elements.sent-2411-250: Furthermore, benign models consistently achieve at least the same degree of collapse as their trojaned counterparts across all metrics.
elements.sent-2411-251: We consider a deep learning classification problem with $K$ classes, $d-$dimensional training samples $\boldsymbol{X _i {= \left\{ x_1, \ldots, x_{n_i \right\}$ for each class $i$, and neural network $f{:}\ \mathbb{R ^d {\to \mathbb{R ^K$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-252: In our experiments this function will be the overlay of a small, solid patch on the base image, as shown in \cref{fig:TrojanAttack_example , or the application of an Instagram filter to the base image, as shown in~\cref{fig:Instagram Filter .}}
elements.subsection-2411-022: Neural Collapse: Metrics and Trojan Impact
elements.sent-2411-253: The observed phenomenon of Neural Collapse can be more precisely described by a set of four characteristics laid out in~\cite{Papyan2020neural and quantified by a standard set of metrics defined in \cite{han2021neural and used in other analyses of NC such as \cite{guo2024cross and \cite{hong2024neural .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-254: In this section we will describe these metrics and present results comparing their convergence over the course of training for both benign and trojaned models on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and GTSRB datasets.
elements.sent-2411-255: Our results show that the application of a trojan trigger causes the NC metrics to converge more slowly and to a larger value than their benign counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-256: There is variation in this behavior across different datasets and different metrics, but in every case the trojaned model shows weaker collapse.

elements.algorithm-2411-003: [H] \caption{Trojan Cleansing with Neural Collapse (ETF-FT)} \label{alg:Cleansing} \begin{algorithmic} \State Input: Trojaned model $h_{\text{troj}}$, clean dataset $\mathbf{X}_{\text{clean}}$ \State \textbf{Step 1: Replace weight matrix with $W_{\text{ETF}}$} \State \text{ } Construct random ETF $W_{\text{ETF}}$ \State \text{ } Replace the final layer weight matrix with $W_{\text{ETF}}$ \State Step 2: Fine-tune the model \State \text{ } Freeze the final layer weight matrix \State \text{ } Fine-tune on the clean dataset $\mathbf{X}_{\text{clean}}$ \State Output: Cleansed model $h_{\text{cleansed}}$ \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-302: We provide experimental evidence showing that trojan attacks significantly weaken NC based on standard metrics.
elements.sent-2411-303: Leveraging these insights, we propose a novel method for trojan cleansing: by fixing the network’s final layer to a simplex ETF—an ideal structure predicted by NC—and fine-tuning on a small amount of clean data.
elements.sent-2411-304: Given the significant experimental evidence of the connection between trojans and Neural Collapse, developing a theoretical framework as well as building a trojan detection method ae important areas of future work.
elements.sent-2411-305: In this work, we used a randomly generated ETF as the starting point in our cleansing strategy; characterizing the impact of the initial ETF selection as well as optimizing this are other interesting areas of investigation.
elements.sent-2411-306: This paper focuses on image classification, but trojans have also been proven effective in other domains such as language and audio.
